content.body:
SUVAT equations (uniform acceleration):
s = ½(u+v)t
v = u + at
s = ut + ½at²
v² = u² + 2as

s=displacement(m), u=initial velocity(m/s)
v=final velocity(m/s), a=acceleration(m/s²), t=time(s)

Gravity: g = 9.8 m/s² (≈10 for estimates)
Free fall: a = g, u = 0 (from rest)

content.title: SUVAT Examples
content.body:
Car starts from rest, accelerates at 3m/s² for 8s.
u=0, a=3, t=8
v=u+at=0+24=24 m/s
s=ut+½at²=0+½(3)(64)=96m

Ball thrown up at 20m/s. Time to reach top:
v=0 (at top), a=-9.8
t=(v-u)/a=(0-20)/(-9.8)=2.04s
Max height: v²=u²+2as → s=u²/(2g)=400/19.6=20.4m

content.title: Velocity-Time Graphs
content.body:
Gradient = acceleration
Area under graph = displacement

Steep gradient: large acceleration
Flat line: constant velocity (a=0)
Line curving: changing acceleration
Negative gradient: deceleration

questions.question: SUVAT: car from rest (u=0), a=5m/s², t=6s. Final speed v=
Distance travelled: u=0, a=4m/s², t=5s
On a velocity-time graph, the area represents
questions.explanation: Area under v-t graph = total displacement (integral of velocity)

questions.question: Gradient of velocity-time graph gives
questions.explanation: Gradient (slope) of v-t graph = Δv/Δt = acceleration

questions.question: Ball dropped from rest. After 3s, velocity (g=10m/s²) =
Ball dropped from rest. Distance fallen in 3s (g=10m/s²) =
questions.explanation: s=½gt²=½×10×9=45m

questions.question: Object decelerates from 25m/s to rest over 5s. Deceleration =
questions.explanation: a=(v-u)/t=(0-25)/5=-5m/s², deceleration=5m/s²

questions.question: Throwing ball vertically up at 20m/s (g=10m/s²). Time to return to same height =
questions.explanation: Time up=20/10=2s, total time=4s (symmetric)

questions.question: Terminal velocity is reached when
questions.explanation: At terminal velocity: drag force = weight → no net force → no acceleration → constant speed

questions.question: What is the SI unit of force?
questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

questions.question: State Newton's Second Law of Motion.
options: An object at rest stays at rest
Every action has an equal reaction
questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law: F = ma, where F is net force, m is mass, and a is acceleration.

questions.question: What is the speed if distance = 60 m and time = 12 s?
questions.explanation: Speed = distance ÷ time = 60 ÷ 12 = 5 m/s.

questions.question: What type of wave is light?
questions.explanation: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave — particles oscillate perpendicular to the direction of travel.

questions.question: What is the unit of electrical resistance?
questions.explanation: Resistance is measured in Ohms (Ω), named after Georg Simon Ohm.

questions.question: Using V = IR, if I = 3A and R = 4Ω, find V.
What is the acceleration due to gravity on Earth?
questions.explanation: Gravitational acceleration on Earth's surface g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (often approximated as 10 m/s² in calculations).

questions.question: What is kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy = ½mv², where m is mass and v is velocity. It is the energy possessed by a moving object.

questions.question: What happens to resistance as temperature increases (for most conductors)?
questions.explanation: For most metallic conductors, as temperature increases, atomic vibrations increase, impeding electron flow, so resistance increases.

questions.question: What is the frequency if period T = 0.5 s?
questions.explanation: Frequency f = 1/T = 1/0.5 = 2 Hz.

content.body:
Newton's Laws:
1st: object at rest stays at rest; moving stays at same velocity, unless net force acts
2nd: F = ma (net force = mass × acceleration)
3rd: Every action has equal and opposite reaction

Weight W = mg
Normal force N (perpendicular to surface)
Friction f = μN (μ = coefficient of friction)

Momentum: p = mv
Impulse = FΔt = Δp = mv - mu
Conservation of momentum: total momentum before = total momentum after (no external forces)

content.title: Newton's 2nd Law Problems
content.body:
Net force 40N on 8kg mass:
a = F/m = 40/8 = 5m/s²

On incline at 30° (mass 5kg):
Component along slope = mg sin30° = 5×10×0.5 = 25N
Normal force = mg cos30° = 5×10×0.866 = 43.3N

content.title: Conservation of Momentum
content.body:
Car A (1000kg, 20m/s) hits stationary car B (1500kg). They stick together.
Before: p = 1000×20 + 1500×0 = 20,000 kg⋅m/s
After: (1000+1500)×v = 20,000
v = 20000/2500 = 8 m/s

questions.question: Newton's 1st law is also called
questions.explanation: 1st law: objects resist changes in motion (inertia) unless acted on by a net force

questions.question: F=ma. Net force 60N on 4kg object. Acceleration =
Newton's 3rd law example: foot pushes ground backward
questions.explanation: Action: foot on ground backward. Reaction: ground on foot forward → walking works

questions.question: Momentum =
questions.explanation: p=mv, units kg⋅m/s

questions.question: Impulse equals change in momentum. F=1000N applied for 0.05s. Impulse =
questions.explanation: Impulse=FΔt=1000×0.05=50 N⋅s = Δp

questions.question: Conservation of momentum applies when
options: no external forces act on system
questions.answer: no external forces act on system
questions.explanation: Momentum is conserved in isolated systems (no external forces)

questions.question: 5kg mass on frictionless surface. Pushed by 25N for 4s. Final velocity (from rest) =
Why do airbags reduce injury in car crashes?
options: They increase collision time → reduce peak force (FΔt=Δp constant)
questions.answer: They increase collision time → reduce peak force (FΔt=Δp constant)
questions.explanation: Same momentum change (impulse) spread over longer time → smaller force. Longer time = lower peak force on body

questions.question: 3kg ball moving 6m/s collides with 2kg ball at rest. They stick. Final velocity =
Normal force on 5kg box on flat surface (g=10m/s²) =
questions.explanation: Normal force = weight on flat surface: N=mg=5×10=50N

content.body:
Projectile: horizontal + vertical motion independent
Horizontal: x = u_x × t  (constant velocity)
Vertical: y = u_y×t - ½gt²  (acceleration g)

Range: R = u²sin(2θ)/g
Max height: H = u²sin²(θ)/2g
Time of flight: T = 2u×sin(θ)/g

content.title: Stone thrown horizontally
content.body:
Stone thrown horizontally at 20m/s from cliff 45m high.
Vertical: 45 = ½×10×t² → t = 3s
Horizontal distance: x = 20 × 3 = 60m
Projectile motion combines uniform horizontal velocity (no air resistance) and uniformly accelerated vertical motion under gravity. Both components are independent and solved separately.

questions.question: In projectile motion, horizontal velocity
questions.explanation: No horizontal force acts (ignoring air resistance), so horizontal velocity remains constant.

questions.question: Vertical component of projectile motion is affected by
questions.explanation: Only gravity acts vertically, giving constant downward acceleration of ~10m/s².

questions.question: Maximum range occurs at launch angle
questions.explanation: Range R = u²sin(2θ)/g is maximum when sin(2θ)=1, i.e., 2θ=90°, θ=45°.

questions.question: Ball thrown horizontally from height h. Time to reach ground =
questions.explanation: Vertical: h = ½gt² → t = sqrt(2h/g)

questions.question: At top of projectile path, vertical velocity is
questions.explanation: At maximum height, vertical velocity momentarily = 0 before reversing direction.

questions.question: Horizontal distance of projectile =
questions.explanation: Range = horizontal speed × time of flight.

questions.question: Time of flight for projectile launched at angle θ with speed u:
questions.explanation: T = 2u sinθ / g (time up = time down by symmetry for level ground).

questions.question: Angles 30° and 60° give
questions.explanation: sin(2×30°) = sin60° = sin(2×60°) = sin120°, so same range but different trajectories.

questions.question: Projectile analysis assumes (2 correct - choose best)
questions.explanation: Standard projectile analysis ignores air resistance and treats horizontal/vertical independently.

questions.question: Speed at bottom after projectile falling height h =
questions.explanation: Using v² = u² + 2as: v² = 0 + 2g×h, v = sqrt(2gh) (for initial vertical velocity=0).

content.body:
Momentum: p = mv  (kg m/s)
Newton's 2nd Law: F = dp/dt = ma

Impulse = F×t = Δp = mv - mu

Conservation of Momentum:
m₁u₁ + m₂u₂ = m₁v₁ + m₂v₂
(closed system, no external forces)

content.title: Collision Problem
content.body:
Car (1000kg, 20m/s) hits stationary car (800kg). They stick together.
1000×20 + 800×0 = 1800×v
v = 20000/1800 = 11.1 m/s
Impulse explains why airbags and crumple zones save lives: by increasing collision time, they reduce average force. Momentum is always conserved in collisions if no external net force acts.

questions.question: Momentum p =
questions.explanation: Momentum = mass × velocity. Units: kg m/s

questions.question: Unit of momentum
questions.explanation: p = mv: kg × m/s = kg m/s

questions.question: Impulse =
questions.explanation: Impulse = F×t. Also equals change in momentum.

questions.question: 1000kg car at 20m/s. Momentum =
Conservation of momentum applies when
questions.explanation: Momentum is conserved in closed system with no external net force.

questions.question: Ball (0.5kg, 10m/s) hits wall and bounces at 8m/s. Change in momentum =
Airbags increase
questions.explanation: Impulse = F×t = Δp. Same impulse over longer time means smaller force.

questions.question: Elastic collision conserves
questions.explanation: Elastic collision: KE conserved (e.g. billiard balls). Inelastic: only momentum conserved.

questions.question: Rocket propulsion uses
questions.explanation: Rocket expels gas backward; reaction pushes rocket forward. Momentum of system is conserved.

content.body: In this lesson, we explore advanced aspects of Kinematics. Build on what you have already learned and apply your knowledge to more challenging problems.
Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson you should be able to: (1) Apply Kinematics concepts to complex problems. (2) Use appropriate formulas and methods. (3) Interpret results in context. (4) Connect this topic to related areas of physics.
Core Theory

This section of Kinematics builds on foundational knowledge. In physics, advanced problem-solving requires not just knowing formulas but understanding when and how to apply them. Study each worked example carefully.

content.title: Worked Example 5.1
content.body: Consider a more complex problem on Kinematics. Approach: (1) Read and understand the problem. (2) Identify all given information. (3) Determine which concepts apply. (4) Solve systematically, showing all steps. (5) Check your answer is reasonable.

content.title: Worked Example 5.2
content.body: Another type of problem you may encounter: Questions may test your ability to combine multiple concepts within Kinematics. Practice identifying the type of problem and the appropriate method before attempting to solve.
Common examination questions on Kinematics include: definitions and explanations, numerical calculations, diagram interpretation, and analysis of given data. Make sure you can handle all types.
Summary and Review

You have now covered all lessons in Kinematics. Review the key concepts from each lesson, practice past paper questions, and ensure you can apply your knowledge confidently. Good luck in your studies!

questions.question: What is the SI unit of force?
questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

questions.question: State Newton's Second Law of Motion.
options: An object at rest stays at rest
Every action has an equal reaction
questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law: F = ma, where F is net force, m is mass, and a is acceleration.

questions.question: What is the speed if distance = 60 m and time = 12 s?
questions.explanation: Speed = distance ÷ time = 60 ÷ 12 = 5 m/s.

questions.question: What type of wave is light?
questions.explanation: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave — particles oscillate perpendicular to the direction of travel.

questions.question: What is the unit of electrical resistance?
questions.explanation: Resistance is measured in Ohms (Ω), named after Georg Simon Ohm.

questions.question: Using V = IR, if I = 3A and R = 4Ω, find V.
What is the acceleration due to gravity on Earth?
questions.explanation: Gravitational acceleration on Earth's surface g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (often approximated as 10 m/s² in calculations).

questions.question: What is kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy = ½mv², where m is mass and v is velocity. It is the energy possessed by a moving object.

questions.question: What happens to resistance as temperature increases (for most conductors)?
questions.explanation: For most metallic conductors, as temperature increases, atomic vibrations increase, impeding electron flow, so resistance increases.

questions.question: What is the frequency if period T = 0.5 s?
questions.explanation: Frequency f = 1/T = 1/0.5 = 2 Hz.

questions.question: Which quantity is a vector?
questions.explanation: Velocity is a vector quantity — it has both magnitude and direction. Speed is a scalar (magnitude only).

questions.question: What is the principle of conservation of energy?
options: Energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transformed
Energy is always lost as heat
questions.answer: Energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transformed
questions.explanation: The law of conservation of energy states that the total energy in a closed system remains constant.

questions.question: What is the unit of power?
questions.explanation: Power is measured in Watts (W). 1 W = 1 J/s. Power is the rate of energy transfer.

questions.question: What is the boiling point of water at standard pressure?
questions.explanation: Water boils at 100°C (373 K) at standard atmospheric pressure (101.325 kPa).

questions.question: In a series circuit, the current:
options: Is the same through all components
Is largest at the first component
Is zero if one component fails
questions.answer: Is the same through all components
questions.explanation: In a series circuit, there is only one path for current, so the same current flows through all components.

questions.question: What is pressure?
questions.explanation: Pressure P = F/A, where F is force applied perpendicular to a surface area A. SI unit is Pascal (Pa).

questions.question: Which type of radiation has the highest penetrating power?
questions.explanation: Gamma rays are electromagnetic radiation with very short wavelength and highest penetrating power, requiring thick lead or concrete to stop.

questions.question: What happens to light when it travels from air into glass?
questions.explanation: Light slows down when entering a denser medium (glass) and changes direction — this is refraction.

questions.question: State the law of conservation of momentum.
options: Total momentum before = total momentum after in a closed system
questions.answer: Total momentum before = total momentum after in a closed system
questions.explanation: In a closed system with no external forces, the total momentum before a collision equals the total momentum after.

questions.question: What is the half-life of a radioactive substance?
options: The time for half the radioactive nuclei to decay
The time for all nuclei to decay
The time to double in activity
questions.answer: The time for half the radioactive nuclei to decay
questions.explanation: Half-life is the time taken for the activity (or number of radioactive nuclei) to decrease to half its original value.
